network.overall_strategy: A multi-environment prebiotic synthesis network for valine (2-amino-3-methylpentanoic acid) centered on four hub molecules: pyruvate, α-ketoisovalerate, isobutyraldehyde, and valine nitrile. The network integrates (1) hydrothermal CO₂ fixation to pyruvate and Fischer-Tropsch-type synthesis of isobutyraldehyde, (2) surface cyanosulfidic photochemistry producing acetaldehyde and building branched C₄–C₅ intermediates via iterative HCN reductive homologation, and (3) biochemical assembly via Strecker hydrolysis and Ni-catalyzed reductive amination. Two major convergence points—α-ketoisovalerate (reached from both pyruvate condensation and cyanohydrin hydrolysis of isobutyraldehyde) and valine nitrile (reached from both Strecker synthesis and cyanosulfidic iterative homologation)—create a densely interconnected and redundant network. Literature-cited high-yield pathways include Ni-catalyzed reductive amination (~50% yield, Kaur et al.), Strecker synthesis from isobutyraldehyde (Miller re-analyses), and Sutherland cyanosulfidic photochemistry with Cu(I)/Cu(II) photoredox cycling.

reactions.name: Hydrothermal CO₂ reduction to pyruvate on iron-sulfur minerals

conditions.other: Alkaline pH 9-11, serpentinization-derived H₂, net reaction: 3CO₂ + 5H₂ → pyruvate + 4H₂O; proceeds via formate and acetyl intermediates on mineral surfaces

reactions.mechanism: Multi-step reductive fixation of CO₂ on Fe₃S₄/Fe₃O₄ surfaces. CO₂ is first reduced to formate via surface-bound hydride transfer, then to CO/formyl intermediates. Sequential C–C coupling on the mineral surface produces a C₃ α-keto acid. The greigite surface acts as an analog of biological [Fe₄S₄] clusters, facilitating the reductive carboxylation of thioacetate intermediates to pyruvate, analogous to the Wood-Ljungdahl pathway.
reactions.reasoning: Greigite and pyrrhotite have been shown to catalyze the reductive carboxylation of thioacetate to pyruvate, a central step in the Wood-Ljungdahl pathway analog (Huber & Wächtershäuser, 1997; Cody et al., 2000). CO₂ hydrogenation to formate on greigite at 100°C has been demonstrated experimentally. Pyruvate is the central metabolic hub of the network and a key precursor to α-keto acids including α-ketoisovalerate. The iron-sulfur world theory (Wächtershäuser, 1988) provides the theoretical framework.

reactions.name: Fischer-Tropsch-type synthesis of isobutyraldehyde on magnetite

conditions.other: Fischer-Tropsch-type conditions with CO₂ first reduced to CO; branched aldehyde is a minor but confirmed product alongside linear alcohols, aldehydes, and hydrocarbons

reactions.mechanism: CO₂ is first reduced to CO on magnetite surfaces via the reverse water-gas shift reaction. CO then undergoes chain growth via methylene insertion on the Fe₃O₄ surface. The branched C₄ aldehyde (isobutyraldehyde, 2-methylpropanal) forms as a minor product when chain growth includes a methyl-branching step. Surface-bound carbene intermediates insert into growing alkyl chains, with branching favored at specific surface sites.
reactions.reasoning: Fischer-Tropsch-type (FTT) synthesis on magnetite in submarine hydrothermal systems has been demonstrated to produce C₁–C₃₅ hydrocarbons, alcohols, and aldehydes (McCollom et al., 1999; Rushdi & Simoneit, 2001). While selectivity for isobutyraldehyde specifically is low and the literature notes that 'the prebiotic synthesis of isobutyraldehyde in high concentrations is less understood,' FTT remains the most plausible hydrothermal source of branched C₄ aldehydes. Isobutyraldehyde is a critical precursor both for valine via Strecker synthesis and for pantetheine/CoA via the Powner pathway.

reactions.name: Pyruvate condensation to acetolactate on pyrite surface

conditions.other: Alkaline pH 9-11; analogous to biological acetolactate synthase; one pyruvate undergoes decarboxylation to form a C₂ carbanion equivalent (hydroxyethyl-TPP analog) that attacks the second pyruvate

reactions.mechanism: One molecule of pyruvate undergoes surface-catalyzed decarboxylation on pyrite, generating a reactive C₂ carbanion (an acetaldehyde equivalent or 'active acetaldehyde' bound to the mineral surface). This nucleophilic C₂ unit attacks the carbonyl carbon of a second pyruvate molecule, forming 2-acetolactate (2-acetyl-2-hydroxypropanoic acid) with release of CO₂. The mineral surface stabilizes the carbanion intermediate and provides a confined reaction environment.
reactions.reasoning: This reaction is the prebiotic analog of the first step in the biological branched-chain amino acid biosynthetic pathway catalyzed by acetolactate synthase (AHAS). Pyrite surfaces have been shown to catalyze decarboxylation and C–C bond formation reactions (Cody et al., 2000, 2004). The iron-sulfur surface acts as a primitive cofactor, stabilizing the reactive intermediates. This condensation provides the critical C₅ skeleton required for valine biosynthesis. Similar aldol-type condensations on mineral surfaces have been demonstrated in hydrothermal analog experiments.

reactions.name: Rearrangement-reduction of acetolactate to α-ketoisovalerate

conditions.other: Alkaline pH 9-11; proceeds via acid-catalyzed 1,2-alkyl migration followed by reduction; may also involve partial dehydration-rehydration on mineral surface; analogous to biological ketol-acid reductoisomerase

reactions.mechanism: Acetolactate undergoes a 1,2-alkyl shift (pinacol-type rearrangement) to form 3-methyl-2-oxobutanoic acid (α-ketoisovalerate). On the greigite surface, the hydroxyl group of acetolactate is activated, facilitating the migration of the methyl-bearing carbon. The resulting intermediate may be further reduced/oxidized to yield the stable α-keto acid. Ni traces in the greigite lattice enhance the rearrangement by providing Lewis acid sites.
reactions.reasoning: In biological systems, acetolactate is converted to α-ketoisovalerate by ketol-acid reductoisomerase (KARI) in a two-step reaction involving an alkyl migration and NADPH-dependent reduction. Prebiotic analogs on mineral surfaces are plausible, as iron-sulfur minerals can catalyze similar rearrangements. The role of Ni in promoting rearrangement reactions on sulfide minerals has been noted in the context of the iron-nickel-sulfur world (Huber & Wächtershäuser, 1997). This step is essential for converting the linear acetolactate skeleton into the branched α-ketoisovalerate required for valine.

reactions.name: Nickel-catalyzed reductive amination of α-ketoisovalerate to valine

conditions.other: pH 11, alkaline conditions, 72 hours reaction time, NH₄⁺/NH₃ concentration >5 mM; highly selective for amino acid over alcohol product when sufficient ammonia present

reactions.mechanism: The carbonyl of α-ketoisovalerate reacts with ammonia to form an imine (Schiff base) intermediate. The imine is then reduced by H₂ activated on the Ni⁰ nanoparticle surface, yielding valine. The Ni surface activates molecular hydrogen via heterolytic cleavage, generating surface-bound hydride that reduces the C=N bond stereoselectively. At pH 11, ammonia is the predominant nitrogen nucleophile.
reactions.reasoning: Kaur et al. demonstrated that Ni nanoparticles catalyze the reductive amination of α-keto acids with ~50% yield for branched-chain amino acids including valine under conditions mimicking serpentinizing alkaline vents (80°C, pH 11, 5 bar H₂, 72 hours). The reaction is highly selective for the amino acid over the corresponding alcohol (isobutanol) when sufficient ammonia is present. H₂ is produced in vast quantities during serpentinization of olivine, making this pathway thermodynamically and geochemically robust. This represents the highest-yield experimentally validated prebiotic route to valine.

reactions.name: Cyanosulfidic UV-driven reduction of HCN to acetaldehyde

conditions.other: UV irradiation (λ < 300 nm, young Sun); H₂S serves as stoichiometric reductant; Cu ions cycle between +1 and +2 oxidation states to mediate electron transfer; neutral pH buffered by phosphate

reactions.mechanism: HCN undergoes UV-driven photoreduction in the presence of H₂S as the sacrificial electron donor. Cu(I) is photooxidized to Cu(II), with the released electron reducing HCN to formaldehyde imine (formaldimine, CH₂=NH) or formaldehyde. Cu(II) is re-reduced to Cu(I) by H₂S, depositing elemental sulfur. Two equivalents of the C₁ reduced products undergo Cu-mediated reductive coupling to form acetaldehyde (C₂). The sulfide mineral surface (stibnite) stabilizes Cu species and provides additional photocatalytic activity.
reactions.reasoning: The Sutherland group demonstrated that HCN and its derivatives undergo UV-driven reductive homologation in the presence of H₂S, producing a cascade of reduced carbon species including aldehydes (Patel et al., 2015; Ritson & Sutherland, 2012). Cu(I)/Cu(II) photoredox cycling is central to this cyanosulfidic network. The literature identifies acetaldehyde and glycolaldehyde as key C₂ intermediates formed in this process. TiO₂ (anatase) on early Earth volcanic surfaces would have provided additional UV photocatalytic capability. Stibnite is identified as a relevant sulfide mineral for cyanosulfidic chemistry.

reactions.name: Cyanosulfidic iterative reductive homologation to valine nitrile

conditions.other: UV irradiation (λ < 300 nm); multi-step iterative process: acetaldehyde undergoes sequential HCN addition, UV-driven H₂S reduction, and amination to build the branched C₅ aminonitrile; neutral pH with phosphate buffering; involves at least 2–3 iterative addition-reduction cycles

reactions.mechanism: Step 1: Acetaldehyde undergoes nucleophilic addition of HCN to form lactonitrile (acetaldehyde cyanohydrin, C₃). Step 2: The nitrile is reduced by H₂S under UV/Cu photoredox conditions to form an aminol or aldehyde (C₃ reduced species including propanal). Step 3: Further HCN addition to the C₃ aldehyde/imine, followed by reductive branching step involving methyl transfer or β-branching, yields the C₅ branched aminonitrile, 2-amino-3-methylbutanenitrile (valine nitrile). The branching selectivity arises from the thermodynamic stability of the isopropyl configuration and the steric environment of the Cu-catalyzed reduction step.
reactions.reasoning: The Sutherland-Patel cyanosulfidic network demonstrates that valine's precursor 2-amino-3-methylbutanenitrile is 'formed through a sequence of iterative HCN additions and reductions' using HCN, H₂S, and UV light with Cu(I)/Cu(II) photoredox (Patel et al., 2015). This multi-step process generates diverse amino acid nitriles simultaneously. The exact intermediates in each iterative cycle are not all fully characterized, but the overall transformation from C₂ aldehyde to C₅ branched aminonitrile via 2–3 homologation cycles is established. This pathway is notable for producing amino acid, nucleotide, and lipid precursors from a common feedstock.

reactions.name: Cyanosulfidic reductive chain extension to isobutyraldehyde

conditions.other: UV irradiation (λ < 300 nm); H₂S is reductant; similar iterative reductive homologation as rxn_007 but stopping at the C₄ aldehyde stage before amination; branched aldehyde product

reactions.mechanism: Acetaldehyde undergoes HCN addition to form lactonitrile (C₃). Under UV/Cu photoredox conditions with H₂S as reductant, the nitrile is reduced to a C₃ aldehyde (propanal or methylformaldehyde). A further HCN addition and reduction cycle extends the chain with branching, yielding the C₄ branched aldehyde isobutyraldehyde (2-methylpropanal). The branching pattern arises from reductive methylation during the chain extension process. This represents the aldehyde branch of the cyanosulfidic network, producing the Strecker precursor for valine.
reactions.reasoning: The cyanosulfidic reductive homologation network (Sutherland and colleagues) produces both aldehydes and aminonitriles depending on the stage at which amination occurs. Isobutyraldehyde is identified in the literature as 'the specific precursor required for valine' in Strecker synthesis and is also 'directly involved in the synthesis of universal cofactors like pantetheine' (Powner et al.). The Powner group demonstrated that isobutyraldehyde selectively reacts within complex cyanosulfidic mixtures to form pantetheine, linking valine precursors to cofactor synthesis. Generating isobutyraldehyde via cyanosulfidic reduction provides a surface-environment source complementary to hydrothermal FTT.

reactions.name: Strecker synthesis: isobutyraldehyde to valine nitrile

conditions.other: Aqueous phase; NH₃ and HCN in solution; clay surface concentrates reactants via adsorption; neutral to slightly alkaline pH; room temperature to 370K; effective in dilute solution due to clay concentration effect

reactions.mechanism: Step 1: Isobutyraldehyde reacts with ammonia to form an imine (Schiff base), (CH₃)₂CHCH=NH, with loss of water. Step 2: Cyanide ion (CN⁻) acts as a nucleophile, attacking the electrophilic carbon of the imine to form 2-amino-3-methylbutanenitrile (valine nitrile). The montmorillonite surface adsorbs and concentrates the aldehyde and amine reactants in its interlayer spaces, increasing effective concentration and promoting the condensation.
reactions.reasoning: The Strecker synthesis is the primary identified pathway for valine formation in Miller-type spark discharge experiments (Miller, 1953, 1957; Johnson et al., 2008 re-analysis). Isobutyraldehyde reacts with NH₃ and HCN in the aqueous phase to form valine nitrile. Re-analysis of Miller's archived samples by LC-ToF-MS confirmed valine among the products. Montmorillonite clay is well-established as a prebiotic catalyst that concentrates organics from dilute solutions (Bernal, 1951; Ferris et al., 1996). The Strecker mechanism for branched amino acids is thermodynamically favorable, with formic acid further lowering barriers for amination (computational studies showing barrier reduction from 22.7 to 13.7 kcal/mol with HCOOH).

reactions.name: Hydrolysis of valine nitrile to valine amide

conditions.other: Slightly acidic to neutral pH (pH 4-7) promotes nitrile hydrolysis; formic acid can significantly lower the activation barrier from 23.2 to 9.3 kcal/mol; slow at neutral pH, accelerated by mild heating or acid catalysis

reactions.mechanism: Water acts as a nucleophile attacking the electrophilic carbon of the nitrile group (C≡N). A tetrahedral intermediate forms, which collapses to release ammonia-equivalent and form the amide bond. The reaction proceeds: R-C≡N + H₂O → R-C(=O)NH₂. Formic acid, when present as a catalytic proton shuttle, dramatically lowers the activation barrier by facilitating proton transfer in the transition state.
reactions.reasoning: Nitrile hydrolysis to amide is the first step in the two-step conversion of Strecker aminonitriles to amino acids. Computational studies (using RTIP sampling) show that formic acid reduces the hydration barrier from 23.2 to 9.3 kcal/mol, making this step kinetically accessible under mild prebiotic conditions. Formic acid is abundantly produced in hydrothermal CO₂ reduction (rxn_001 products), providing a natural catalyst for this step. The reaction is well-characterized in the context of Miller-Urey chemistry and Strecker synthesis (Miller, 1957; Cleaves et al., 2008).

reactions.name: Hydrolysis of valine amide to valine

conditions.other: Acidic or alkaline conditions accelerate hydrolysis; pH < 4 or pH > 10 optimal; proceeds slowly at neutral pH over days to weeks; thermal acceleration at 350-370K

reactions.mechanism: The amide undergoes nucleophilic addition of water (or hydroxide at high pH) to the carbonyl carbon of the amide group, forming a tetrahedral intermediate. Collapse of this intermediate releases ammonia and yields the free carboxylic acid, producing valine (2-amino-3-methylbutanoic acid). Under acidic conditions, the amide nitrogen is protonated to form a better leaving group.
reactions.reasoning: Amide hydrolysis to carboxylic acid is the final step of the classical Strecker amino acid synthesis. This reaction is well-established in prebiotic chemistry literature (Miller, 1957; Schlesinger & Miller, 1983). In prebiotic pools, cycles of pH change (e.g., mixing of alkaline vent fluids with acidic ocean water) would periodically accelerate this hydrolysis. The reaction is thermodynamically favorable (ΔG < 0) in aqueous solution and proceeds to completion over timescales of days to weeks under mildly acidic conditions.

reactions.name: Cyanohydrin formation from isobutyraldehyde

conditions.other: Slightly alkaline pH favors CN⁻ formation; rapid equilibrium reaction; cyanohydrin is kinetically accessible intermediate; competes with Strecker imine pathway depending on NH₃ availability

reactions.mechanism: In the absence of sufficient ammonia, or when CN⁻ attacks the aldehyde directly before imine formation, isobutyraldehyde undergoes nucleophilic addition of cyanide to the carbonyl carbon, forming 2-hydroxy-3-methylbutanenitrile (the cyanohydrin). This reaction is rapid and reversible, with the equilibrium favoring product formation at pH 8-9 where CN⁻ concentration is significant.
reactions.reasoning: Cyanohydrin formation from aldehydes and HCN is one of the most fundamental reactions in prebiotic chemistry. It represents the alternative branch point from isobutyraldehyde: when NH₃ is present, Strecker aminonitrile forms (rxn_009); when NH₃ is absent or at low concentration, the cyanohydrin forms instead. This provides a route from the aldehyde hub to the α-keto acid hub via hydrolysis and oxidation (rxn_013). Cyanohydrin chemistry is well-established in the prebiotic literature as a source of α-hydroxy acids, which are abundant in meteorites alongside amino acids (Peltzer & Bada, 1978).

reactions.name: Hydrolysis and UV oxidation of cyanohydrin to α-ketoisovalerate

conditions.other: Two-step process: (1) nitrile hydrolysis to α-hydroxyisovaleric acid (300-350K, mild acid/base, days); (2) UV-driven oxidation of α-hydroxy acid to α-keto acid on TiO₂ surface (λ < 350 nm); net: cyanohydrin → α-hydroxyisovaleric acid → α-ketoisovalerate

reactions.mechanism: Step 1 (Hydrolysis): The nitrile group of 2-hydroxy-3-methylbutanenitrile undergoes hydrolysis (nitrile → amide → acid), yielding 2-hydroxy-3-methylbutanoic acid (α-hydroxyisovaleric acid). Step 2 (Oxidation): The α-hydroxy acid is oxidized at the α-carbon by UV light on TiO₂ photocatalytic surfaces, converting the –CHOH– group to –CO– and yielding 3-methyl-2-oxobutanoic acid (α-ketoisovalerate). TiO₂ generates reactive oxygen species under UV that selectively oxidize α-hydroxy acids.
reactions.reasoning: α-Hydroxy acids are found alongside amino acids in carbonaceous chondrites, confirming their prebiotic relevance (Peltzer & Bada, 1978; Pizzarello et al., 2004). The hydrolysis of cyanohydrins to α-hydroxy acids is well-established. Subsequent oxidation to α-keto acids on TiO₂ photocatalytic surfaces is plausible given the abundance of TiO₂ (anatase/rutile) on early Earth volcanic terrain. This reaction provides a critical bridge between the isobutyraldehyde hub and the α-ketoisovalerate hub, connecting FTT and cyanosulfidic aldehyde pathways to the reductive amination route. This topology was identified as missing in the previous network attempt.

reactions.name: UV photolysis of ammonia to hydroxylamine

conditions.other: UV irradiation λ < 230 nm (strong UV flux on early Earth without ozone layer); low yield but continuous production; NH₃ + hν → NH₂• + H•; NH₂• + OH• → NH₂OH; TiO₂ enhances photolysis rate

reactions.mechanism: UV photons (λ < 230 nm) homolytically cleave the N–H bond of ammonia, generating amidogen radical (NH₂•) and hydrogen radical (H•). Simultaneously, UV photolysis of water generates hydroxyl radical (OH•). Radical recombination of NH₂• and OH• yields hydroxylamine (NH₂OH). TiO₂ surfaces enhance the process by generating reactive radical species via photocatalytic water splitting.
reactions.reasoning: UV photolysis of NH₃ in aqueous solution to produce hydroxylamine is a known photochemical reaction. On early Earth, the absence of an ozone layer allowed deep UV (< 230 nm) to reach the surface. The literature identifies hydroxylamine as a reactive nitrogen species for amino acid synthesis in supercritical CO₂/H₂O systems. TiO₂ (anatase) is an effective UV photocatalyst found in volcanic terrain. While yields are low per photon, continuous UV exposure over geological timescales provides a steady supply of NH₂OH.

reactions.name: Hydroxylamine amination of α-ketoisovalerate to valine

conditions.other: Supercritical CO₂/liquid H₂O biphasic system mimicking 'crust soda fountains'; NH₂OH is a more reactive nitrogen nucleophile than NH₃; polymerization of products also observed (free amino acid:polymer ratio ~1:3 at 60°C)

reactions.mechanism: Hydroxylamine (NH₂OH) attacks the carbonyl carbon of α-ketoisovalerate, forming an oxime intermediate (C=NOH). Under the high-pressure CO₂/H₂O conditions, the oxime undergoes reduction (facilitated by the reducing environment) and rearrangement to form the α-amino acid valine. Alternatively, the oxime can undergo a Beckmann-type rearrangement and hydrolysis to yield the amino acid. The supercritical CO₂ phase creates a unique reaction environment that promotes both monomer formation and subsequent polymerization.
reactions.reasoning: Synthesis experiments using supercritical CO₂/liquid H₂O systems simulating 'crust soda fountains' have produced amino acids from hydroxylamine and keto acids at 60°C. The literature reports that alanine and glycine were detected, with valine identified as a likely product in expanded keto acid trials. These systems also promote polymer formation with a ~1:3 ratio of free amino acids to polymers. This pathway represents an alternative amination route distinct from both Ni-catalyzed reductive amination (rxn_005) and Strecker synthesis.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal protometabolic pathway: CO₂ → pyruvate → acetolactate → α-ketoisovalerate → valine (Ni reductive amination)
pathways.description: Complete hydrothermal route mimicking the biological branched-chain amino acid biosynthetic pathway. CO₂ is fixed to pyruvate on greigite/magnetite, two pyruvates condense to acetolactate on pyrite, acetolactate rearranges to α-ketoisovalerate, and Ni nanoparticles catalyze reductive amination to valine with ~50% yield under serpentinizing vent conditions (80°C, pH 11, 5 bar H₂).

pathways.name: Hydrothermal FTT + Surface Strecker pathway
pathways.description: Isobutyraldehyde is generated by Fischer-Tropsch-type synthesis on magnetite in hydrothermal vents, then transported to surface environments where it undergoes Strecker synthesis with NH₃ and HCN on montmorillonite clay, followed by sequential nitrile hydrolysis to valine.

pathways.name: Cyanosulfidic direct aminonitrile pathway (Sutherland-Patel)
pathways.description: HCN is UV-reduced to acetaldehyde via H₂S-driven cyanosulfidic photochemistry with Cu(I)/Cu(II) photoredox cycling. Acetaldehyde then undergoes iterative HCN additions and reductions to build the branched C₅ aminonitrile (valine nitrile) directly, followed by two-step hydrolysis to valine. This systems chemistry approach produces diverse biomolecule precursors simultaneously.

pathways.name: Cyanosulfidic aldehyde + Strecker combination
pathways.description: Cyanosulfidic photochemistry generates both acetaldehyde and then isobutyraldehyde via reductive chain extension. The isobutyraldehyde then enters the classical Strecker pathway with NH₃ and HCN on clay surfaces. This pathway links cyanosulfidic C–C bond construction to classical Strecker aminonitrile formation.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal protometabolic + hydroxylamine amination
pathways.description: The α-ketoisovalerate produced by the protometabolic hydrothermal pathway (CO₂ → pyruvate → acetolactate → α-KIV) is aminated by hydroxylamine (generated by UV photolysis of NH₃ on TiO₂) under supercritical CO₂ conditions, bypassing the need for Ni catalyst and H₂ pressure.

pathways.name: FTT aldehyde → cyanohydrin → α-keto acid → Ni reductive amination
pathways.description: Isobutyraldehyde from hydrothermal FTT synthesis undergoes cyanohydrin formation with HCN on the surface, hydrolysis/UV oxidation to α-ketoisovalerate on TiO₂, and then returns to hydrothermal vent conditions for Ni-catalyzed reductive amination to valine. This pathway bridges the isobutyraldehyde and α-ketoisovalerate hubs.

pathways.name: Cyanosulfidic aldehyde → cyanohydrin → α-keto acid → Ni reductive amination
pathways.description: Full surface-to-hydrothermal pathway: HCN is photoreduced to acetaldehyde, then extended to isobutyraldehyde via cyanosulfidic chemistry. The isobutyraldehyde undergoes cyanohydrin formation and oxidation to α-ketoisovalerate on the surface, which is then aminated to valine by Ni catalysis in hydrothermal vents.

pathways.name: FTT aldehyde → cyanohydrin → α-keto acid → hydroxylamine amination
pathways.description: Isobutyraldehyde from FTT is converted to α-ketoisovalerate via the cyanohydrin route (rxn_012 → rxn_013), then aminated by hydroxylamine under supercritical CO₂ conditions. Hydroxylamine is independently generated by UV photolysis of NH₃ (rxn_014). All surface/biochemical steps after initial hydrothermal FTT.

pathways.name: Cyanosulfidic aldehyde → cyanohydrin → α-keto acid → hydroxylamine amination
pathways.description: Entirely surface-based origin of valine: HCN-derived acetaldehyde is extended to isobutyraldehyde, converted to α-ketoisovalerate via cyanohydrin hydrolysis/oxidation, and aminated by UV-generated hydroxylamine. No hydrothermal steps required—demonstrating that surface geochemistry alone can produce valine.

pathways.name: Convergent multi-environment pathway through dual α-ketoisovalerate sources
pathways.description: Demonstrates the convergence of hydrothermal and surface chemistry at the α-ketoisovalerate hub. Two independent routes produce α-KIV: (1) hydrothermal pyruvate condensation (rxn_001 → rxn_003 → rxn_004) and (2) surface cyanohydrin oxidation of FTT-derived isobutyraldehyde (rxn_002 → rxn_012 → rxn_013). Both converge at α-KIV, which is then aminated by the highest-yield Ni reductive amination (rxn_005). This represents the most geochemically robust route due to multiple independent upstream supply chains.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Protometabolic pathway mirrors biological valine biosynthesis (pyruvate condensation → acetolactate → α-ketoisovalerate). The final Ni-catalyzed reductive amination is very well supported (~50% yield, Kaur et al.). However, rxn_004 (acetolactate rearrangement-reduction without enzymatic thiamine pyrophosphate cofactor) is the weakest link—the prebiotic analog of acetohydroxy acid isomeroreductase on mineral surfaces remains speculative. rxn_003 (pyruvate self-condensation to acetolactate on pyrite) is plausible but yields would likely be low without thiamine catalysis.
suggestions: Specify a mineral or metal ion catalyst for the acetolactate rearrangement (e.g., Zn²⁺ or Fe²⁺ on clay)
Consider adding thiamine or a primitive thiazole cofactor as a catalytic agent for rxn_003
Provide temperature/pressure conditions for each hydrothermal step

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Strong pathway combining hydrothermal FTT synthesis with the classic Strecker mechanism. The environmental progression (Hydrothermal → Surface → Biochemical) is exemplary. Strecker synthesis from isobutyraldehyde is directly supported by Miller re-analyses. The main concern is the selectivity of FTT synthesis for branched C4 aldehyde (isobutyraldehyde vs. n-butyraldehyde and other products)—FTT typically produces Anderson-Schulz-Flory distributions favoring linear products. The Strecker and hydrolysis steps are textbook prebiotic chemistry.
suggestions: Address selectivity for branched vs. linear C4 aldehydes in FTT synthesis
Specify magnetite surface area/grain size and T/P conditions
Consider adding a branching mechanism or aldol step to explain isobutyraldehyde formation

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Well-aligned with Sutherland-Patel cyanosulfidic chemistry. The compression of iterative reductive homologation from acetaldehyde to valine nitrile (rxn_007) is the main weakness—this involves multiple HCN additions, reductions, and a branching step, all compressed into one reaction node. The Cu(I)/Cu(II) photoredox cycling is well-supported. The environmental flow (Surface → Biochemical) is coherent. Literature confirms that cyanosulfidic networks can generate broad BCAA suites.
suggestions: Decompose rxn_007 into explicit intermediate steps showing C3 and C4 nitrile intermediates
Specify Cu(I)/Cu(II) and phosphate buffer as catalysts explicitly
Address selectivity for branched vs. linear C5 aminonitrile

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Elegant combination of cyanosulfidic aldehyde production feeding into classic Strecker synthesis. The route through isobutyraldehyde as a defined intermediate is chemically cleaner than the direct homologation in P3. The chain extension rxn_008 (C2→C4 branched) still requires explanation of branching selectivity. Environmental progression is smooth: Surface photochemistry generates aldehyde, Strecker occurs in aqueous surface pools, hydrolysis proceeds under mild conditions.
suggestions: Detail the branching mechanism—is it via aldol condensation of two C2 units?
Specify UV wavelength range and H₂S concentration for optimal yields

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Combines the protometabolic early steps with the hydroxylamine amination route. While the supercritical CO₂ literature supports hydroxylamine-keto acid reactions, the UV photolysis of NH₃ to hydroxylamine (rxn_014) under realistic surface conditions may produce low yields. The acetolactate rearrangement (rxn_004) remains the weakest step. The hydroxylamine amination (rxn_015) is less established than the Ni reductive amination for valine specifically.
suggestions: Provide experimental yields or references for hydroxylamine production from NH₃ photolysis
Consider alternative hydroxylamine sources (e.g., NO reduction)
Strengthen evidence for rxn_015 selectivity toward valine vs. oxime formation

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Interesting convergent pathway linking FTT synthesis through cyanohydrin to α-ketoisovalerate, which then feeds into the high-yield Ni reductive amination. The cyanohydrin formation is textbook chemistry. The cyanohydrin-to-α-keto acid conversion (rxn_013) requiring UV oxidation is plausible but less established—typically cyanohydrin hydrolysis yields α-hydroxy acid, and subsequent oxidation to α-keto acid needs a specific oxidant. The back-transition from Surface to Hydrothermal for rxn_005 is somewhat unconventional but physically plausible (e.g., surface runoff into vent systems).
suggestions: Clarify the oxidation mechanism in rxn_013—MnO₂ or UV/Fe³⁺?
Justify the Surface → Hydrothermal reverse flow geologically
Consider whether Fe³⁺ minerals could serve as the oxidant

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Longer but chemically diverse pathway. Builds isobutyraldehyde through cyanosulfidic chemistry, then converts via cyanohydrin to α-ketoisovalerate for Ni reductive amination. The number of environment transitions (Surface → Surface → Hydrothermal) and the cyanohydrin oxidation step are concerns. The strong final step partially compensates.
suggestions: Reduce pathway length if possible
Provide stronger evidence for cyanohydrin oxidation under prebiotic conditions

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Combines FTT aldehyde synthesis with cyanohydrin-keto acid conversion and hydroxylamine amination. Environmental flow is reasonable (Hydrothermal → Surface → Biochemical) but accumulates weaker reactions. Neither the FTT selectivity for branched aldehyde nor the hydroxylamine amination is individually strong enough to carry the pathway.
suggestions: This pathway combines too many speculative steps
Consider replacing hydroxylamine route with established Strecker or reductive amination

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Longest pathway at 6 steps. While each step is individually plausible, the cumulative yield through 6 steps with several moderate-confidence reactions would be very low. The pathway exists primarily for network connectivity rather than as a practical synthesis route.
suggestions: This pathway is valuable for network redundancy but not as a primary route
Focus optimization efforts on shorter pathways

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This convergent pathway demonstrates the network's robustness by producing α-ketoisovalerate from both protometabolic (pyruvate condensation) and cyanohydrin hydrolysis routes. While not the most efficient individual route, it exemplifies the convergent design philosophy. The dual sourcing of the key hub intermediate is a significant strength for network resilience.
suggestions: This meta-pathway is valuable for network characterization
Document the relative flux through each convergent arm under various conditions

evaluation.network_feedback: This is a well-designed, literature-grounded synthesis network for valine with strong topology and multiple convergent pathways. Key strengths: (1) Excellent alignment with three major literature-supported routes—Ni-catalyzed reductive amination (Kaur et al., ~50% yield), Strecker synthesis from isobutyraldehyde (Miller re-analyses), and Sutherland cyanosulfidic photochemistry. (2) Four well-chosen hub molecules (pyruvate, α-ketoisovalerate, isobutyraldehyde, valine nitrile) that create genuine convergence, not merely serial connectivity. (3) Good environmental flow in the strongest pathways (Hydrothermal → Surface → Biochemical). (4) Redundancy ensures robustness—the target is reachable via at least 3 pathways with confidence ≥0.80. Key weaknesses: (1) The acetolactate rearrangement to α-ketoisovalerate (rxn_004) lacks strong prebiotic evidence without thiamine catalysis. (2) FTT selectivity for branched C4 aldehyde is not well-established. (3) Some multi-step processes are compressed into single reaction nodes (rxn_007, rxn_008). (4) The cyanohydrin-to-α-keto acid oxidation (rxn_013) needs a clearer oxidant specification. (5) A few surface reactions lack explicit mineral catalyst specifications. Despite these issues, the network successfully integrates three distinct geochemical environments with appropriate chemistry, and 3 of 10 pathways achieve approval threshold. The overall architecture represents a sophisticated and scientifically credible model for prebiotic valine synthesis.
